subject: Social Science
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 5 periods = 200 minutes
  Row 2: 60 minutes × 1 period = 60 minutes
Total: 6 periods · 4h 20min

result.lesson_plan:
---

### Period 1 — 40 minutes
**Activity: Mapping India's Regional Mosaic**
*Section anchor: Introduction: India as a Subcontinent*
*Competency: C-7.2 (Central)*
*Materials: Outline map of India (one per student), coloured pencils*

| Time | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 0–5 min | Teacher poses the question: "If you had to divide India into zones based on what the land looks like, what would you create?" — students write two or three predictions individually. |
| 5–15 min | Students read the Introduction section and identify the five broad geographical regions named in the text, annotating each on their outline map with a distinct colour. |
| 15–25 min | In pairs, students compare their maps and discuss: which region covers the largest area visually, and which borders act as natural barriers — they label at least three natural boundaries (Himalayas, Thar, Bay of Bengal, Indian Ocean, Arabian Sea). |
| 25–35 min | Each pair shares one observation with the class; teacher scribes a collective list of "what the land does" — separates, connects, feeds, protects — drawing out that geography is not neutral. |
| 35–40 min | Students write one sentence: "India's regions differ because…" completing it with evidence from the map they built. |

**Implied LO:** Students will be able to locate and label India's five geographical regions on an outline map and justify at least one way each natural boundary shapes the character of the region it borders.

---

### Period 2 — 40 minutes
**Activity: Himalayas — Forces That Built Them**
*Section anchor: Formation of the Himalayas; Three Main Ranges of the Himalayas*
*Competency: C-6.1 (Substantive)*
*Materials: Carpet/cloth strip for demonstration (optional), textbook*

| Time | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 0–5 min | Teacher asks: "Can mountains grow? How would you prove it?" — students give brief oral responses. |
| 5–15 min | Students read the Formation section, then use the carpet-push demonstration (or sketch a cross-section diagram) to show how Gondwana's breakup and continental collision produced the fold mountains. |
| 15–25 min | Students construct a three-column table comparing Himadri, Himachal, and Shivalik Hills across: altitude/character, permanent snow cover, and human settlement — drawing all data from the Three Main Ranges section. |
| 25–35 min | Students apply the table: "A family wants to build a hill station. Which range would they choose, and which would they avoid? Write two sentences for each choice using your table as evidence." |
| 35–40 min | Whole-class share-out; teacher connects Himalaya's ongoing growth (5 mm/year) to the fact that the process that built them is still active. |

**Implied LO:** Students will be able to construct a comparative table of the three Himalayan ranges and use it to justify a human settlement decision with evidence from the chapter.

---

### Period 3 — 40 minutes
**Activity: Rivers, Deserts, and Survival Choices**
*Section anchor: The Himalayas as a Water Tower; The Great Indian Desert — Thar Desert*
*Competency: C-6.4 (Present)*
*Materials: None required*

| Time | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 0–5 min | Teacher presents two brief scenarios on the board: (A) a farmer in the Gangetic Plains in June; (B) a family in Jaisalmer in June — students predict what each person's biggest concern is. |
| 5–15 min | Students read the Water Tower and Thar Desert sections, then revisit their predictions: which were correct, which need revision, and what evidence in the text supports the revision. |
| 15–25 min | Students complete a structured contrast: "The Himalayas give water by ___ ; the Thar withholds water because ___. People in the Gangetic Plains respond by ___; people in the Thar respond by ___." Students fill each blank using only textual evidence. |
| 25–35 min | Discussion: "Both regions have people living successfully. What does that tell us about the relationship between geography and human adaptation?" Students articulate at least one inference. |
| 35–40 min | Exit task: students name one livelihood practice from the Thar section and explain in one sentence how the physical geography of the desert makes that practice necessary. |

**Implied LO:** Students will be able to identify at least one livelihood adaptation from the Thar Desert section and explain, in writing, the specific geographical condition that makes it necessary.

---

### Period 4 — 40 minutes
**Activity: Plateau, Coast, and River Logic**
*Section anchor: The Peninsular Plateau; India's Amazing Coastlines*
*Competency: C-7.2 (Central)*
*Materials: Outline map of peninsular India (one per student)*

| Time | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 0–5 min | Students recall from Period 1: which region sits at the south of their regional map? Teacher focuses attention: "Today we go inside that triangle." |
| 5–15 min | Students read the Peninsular Plateau section and mark on their maps: Western Ghats, Eastern Ghats, Deccan Plateau, and arrows showing the eastward tilt and the direction the major rivers flow. |
| 15–25 min | Students analyse the tilt-and-flow logic: "The plateau tilts east — so rivers flow east — except Narmada and Tapti. Why might two rivers break the pattern?" Students propose a hypothesis drawing on the text. |
| 25–35 min | Students read the Coastlines section and add the West Coast and East Coast to their maps, labelling two distinguishing features of each (estuaries vs. deltas; short swift rivers vs. wide plains). |
| 35–40 min | Students write: "The Eastern and Western coasts differ because the land behind each coast is different. Explain this with two pieces of map evidence." |

**Implied LO:** Students will be able to annotate a map of the Peninsular Plateau and coastlines showing river-flow direction, and explain in writing the geographical reason rivers flow differently on each side.

---

### Period 5 — 40 minutes
**Activity: Cold Desert and Island Extremes**
*Section anchor: The Cold Desert of India — Ladakh; Indian Islands; The Delta in West Bengal and the Sundarbans*
*Competency: C-6.1 (Substantive)*
*Materials: None required*

| Time | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 0–5 min | Teacher poses: "Ladakh gets almost no rain. The Sundarbans are flooded regularly. What single force could explain how both landscapes were created?" — students speculate briefly. |
| 5–15 min | Students read the Ladakh section and identify the two-step formation story: tectonic uplift of ancient ocean floor + wind and water erosion. They write the two steps in their own words. |
| 15–25 min | Students read the Sundarbans and Islands sections; they identify the formation process in each — delta formation by three rivers, coral island formation, volcanic island formation — and classify each as: tectonic, depositional, or erosional. |
| 25–35 min | Students compare Ladakh and the Sundarbans: same country, opposite landscape characters — students write three contrasting factual statements, then one unifying statement: "Both exist because…" |
| 35–40 min | Whole-class share: "Which of these landscapes was most surprising to you, and which piece of evidence in the chapter changed your initial idea about it?" |

**Implied LO:** Students will be able to classify the formation process of Ladakh, the Sundarbans, and the island groups as tectonic, depositional, or erosional, and justify each classification with textual evidence.

---

### Period 6 — 60 minutes
**Activity: India's Geography — Source Analysis and Synthesis**
*Section anchor: The Hills of the Northeast; The Aravalli Hills; full chapter integration*
*Competency: C-7.2 (Central) with C-6.1 integration*
*Materials: Printed source cards (two short excerpts: one on Mawlynnong's root bridges, one on Zawar zinc mines — drawn from chapter text; teacher prepares in advance)*

| Time | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 0–10 min | Teacher distributes source cards. Students read both excerpts silently and write one question each excerpt raises for them about the relationship between landscape and human activity. |
| 10–20 min | Students read the Northeast Hills and Aravalli Hills sections in full; they identify which region each source card belongs to and what the chapter adds beyond what the card alone says. |
| 20–35 min | Students complete a structured comparison table: Northeast Hills vs. Aravallis — rows: age and formation type, ecological role, human communities and livelihoods, one distinctive feature. They use both source cards and chapter text as evidence. |
| 35–50 min | Synthesis writing task: "India's geographical diversity is not just a list of different landforms — it is a story of how the same forces produced very different landscapes and very different ways of life. Using at least three regions from this chapter, write a short explanation that supports this claim." (Minimum: three regions, two formation processes, two examples of human adaptation.) |
| 50–60 min | Pair exchange: each student reads their partner's explanation and identifies one region-claim pair that is well-supported and one that needs stronger evidence — oral feedback given, no written mark. |

**Implied LO:** Students will be able to produce a written explanation using evidence from at least three chapter regions to support the claim that India's topographical diversity reflects both geological processes and human adaptation.

---

lo_handoff.activity_name: Students colour-code and annotate an outline map of India's five geographical regions, identify natural boundaries, and write a sentence explaining why regions differ.
lo_handoff.activity_summary: Students colour-code and annotate an outline map of India's five geographical regions, identify natural boundaries, and write a sentence explaining why regions differ.
lo_handoff.implied_lo: Students will be able to locate and label India's five geographical regions on an outline map and justify at least one way each natural boundary shapes the character of the region it borders.
lo_handoff.competency_text: Discovers the topographical diversity of the Indian landmass — from the semi-arid zone in the west and the areas of heavy rains in the north-east to the long coastal areas in the south and the snow-clad mountains in the north, as well as the rich biodiversity of the country

lo_handoff.chapter_section: Formation of the Himalayas; Three Main Ranges of the Himalayas
lo_handoff.activity_name: Students model Himalayan formation via the Gondwana-collision explanation, construct a three-column comparison table of the three ranges, and apply it to justify a human settlement decision.
lo_handoff.activity_summary: Students model Himalayan formation via the Gondwana-collision explanation, construct a three-column comparison table of the three ranges, and apply it to justify a human settlement decision.
lo_handoff.implied_lo: Students will be able to construct a comparative table of the three Himalayan ranges and use it to justify a human settlement decision with evidence from the chapter.
lo_handoff.competency_text: Explains key natural phenomena such as climate, weather, ocean cycles, soil formation, the flow of rivers, and how they are spatially distributed

lo_handoff.chapter_section: The Himalayas as a Water Tower; The Great Indian Desert — Thar Desert
lo_handoff.activity_name: Students contrast the Himalayan water-supply system with Thar Desert water-scarcity conditions, complete a structured fill-in contrast, and explain one Thar livelihood adaptation in writing.
lo_handoff.activity_summary: Students contrast the Himalayan water-supply system with Thar Desert water-scarcity conditions, complete a structured fill-in contrast, and explain one Thar livelihood adaptation in writing.
lo_handoff.implied_lo: Students will be able to identify at least one livelihood adaptation from the Thar Desert section and explain, in writing, the specific geographical condition that makes it necessary.
lo_handoff.competency_text: Correlates the existence of different patterns of livelihoods with different types of landforms, availability of resources, and climatic conditions and changes in local, regional, national, and global contexts

lo_handoff.chapter_section: The Peninsular Plateau; India's Amazing Coastlines
lo_handoff.activity_name: Students map the Peninsular Plateau's tilt, river-flow direction, and both coastlines, then write a geographical explanation for why Eastern and Western coasts have different characters.
lo_handoff.activity_summary: Students map the Peninsular Plateau's tilt, river-flow direction, and both coastlines, then write a geographical explanation for why Eastern and Western coasts have different characters.
lo_handoff.implied_lo: Students will be able to annotate a map of the Peninsular Plateau and coastlines showing river-flow direction, and explain in writing the geographical reason rivers flow differently on each side.
lo_handoff.competency_text: Discovers the topographical diversity of the Indian landmass — from the semi-arid zone in the west and the areas of heavy rains in the north-east to the long coastal areas in the south and the snow-clad mountains in the north, as well as the rich biodiversity of the country

lo_handoff.chapter_section: The Cold Desert of India — Ladakh; Indian Islands; The Delta in West Bengal and the Sundarbans
lo_handoff.activity_name: Students identify formation processes for Ladakh, the Sundarbans, and Indian island groups, classify each as tectonic/depositional/erosional, and write contrasting and unifying statements about India's extreme landscapes.
lo_handoff.activity_summary: Students identify formation processes for Ladakh, the Sundarbans, and Indian island groups, classify each as tectonic/depositional/erosional, and write contrasting and unifying statements about India's extreme landscapes.
lo_handoff.implied_lo: Students will be able to classify the formation process of Ladakh, the Sundarbans, and the island groups as tectonic, depositional, or erosional, and justify each classification with textual evidence.
lo_handoff.competency_text: Explains key natural phenomena such as climate, weather, ocean cycles, soil formation, the flow of rivers, and how they are spatially distributed

lo_handoff.chapter_section: The Hills of the Northeast; The Aravalli Hills; full chapter integration
lo_handoff.activity_name: Students analyse two short source cards drawn from chapter content on the Northeast Hills and Aravallis, complete a structured comparison table, and write a multi-region synthesis explanation linking topographical diversity to geological processes and human adaptation.
lo_handoff.activity_summary: Students analyse two short source cards drawn from chapter content on the Northeast Hills and Aravallis, complete a structured comparison table, and write a multi-region synthesis explanation linking topographical diversity to geological processes and human adaptation.
lo_handoff.implied_lo: Students will be able to produce a written explanation using evidence from at least three chapter regions to support the claim that India's topographical diversity reflects both geological processes and human adaptation.
lo_handoff.competency_text: Discovers the topographical diversity of the Indian landmass — from the semi-arid zone in the west and the areas of heavy rains in the north-east to the long coastal areas in the south and the snow-clad mountains in the north, as well as the rich biodiversity of the country

result.assessment:
## Assessment

*Chapter: Geographical Diversity of India | Grade VII | Social Sciences*
*Assessment is chapter-scoped and formative. All questions are traceable to the LO Handoff above.*

---

### Central Competency — C-7.2
*(Discovering India's topographical diversity through systematic regional survey)*

---

**MCQ 1**

India's coastline shows distinct differences between the West Coast and the East Coast. Which of the following correctly explains why the East Coast has large fertile deltas while the West Coast has estuaries?

A. The East Coast receives more rainfall than the West Coast.
B. ★ Rivers on the East Coast travel longer distances across flat land and deposit sediment, while West Coast rivers are short and swift and carry sediment into tidal inlets.
C. The Eastern Ghats are taller than the Western Ghats, pushing rivers to deposit more material.
D. The Bay of Bengal is calmer than the Arabian Sea, allowing deltas to form more easily.

> **Annotation (Full)**
> *LO citation:* Period 4 — "Students will be able to annotate a map of the Peninsular Plateau and coastlines showing river-flow direction, and explain in writing the geographical reason rivers flow differently on each side."
> *Competency link:* C-7.2 — identifying topographical diversity through systematic regional comparison.
> *Spectrum position:* Application — student must connect plateau tilt and river-length logic to coastal landform type.
> *Purpose:* Tests whether the student can apply the tilt-and-flow mechanism from the map activity to explain a coastal landform difference.
> *Distractor A:* Indicates the student linked coastal character to rainfall rather than to river behaviour — a content conflation common when students read about the Western Ghats' role in rainfall without connecting it to river length and load.
> *Distractor C:* Indicates the student reversed the Ghat height relationship — the Western Ghats are taller, not the Eastern; selecting this shows incomplete range comparison.
> *Distractor D:* Indicates the student used sea-condition reasoning rather than land-and-river reasoning — a plausible but geography-free inference.
> *Differentiation note:* A student who received oral delivery of the Coastlines section may answer correctly but be unable to explain the mechanism — a one-sentence written justification distinguishes genuine engagement from recall.
> *Oral follow-up:* "Point to where on your map the rivers that form these deltas begin — what does that tell you about how far they travel?"

---

**MCQ 2**

Which of the following best explains why the Gangetic Plains support a much denser population than the Thar Desert, using evidence from the chapter?

A. The Gangetic Plains have more cities and better roads, so more people moved there over time.
B. The Thar Desert has extreme temperatures that make permanent settlement impossible.
C. ★ Himalayan meltwater makes the Gangetic Plains' soil fertile and water-supplied, while the Thar Desert's lack of water and extreme heat limit both agriculture and movement.
D. The Indian government invested more in the Gangetic Plains region than in Rajasthan.

> **Annotation (Full)**
> *LO citation:* Period 1 — "…justify at least one way each natural boundary shapes the character of the region it borders"; Period 3 — "…identify at least one livelihood adaptation from the Thar Desert section and explain the specific geographical condition that makes it necessary."
> *Competency link:* C-7.2 — understanding topographical regions through their distinguishing geographical conditions.
> *Spectrum position:* Analysis — student must weigh two contrasting regional sets of conditions and identify the causal mechanism.
> *Purpose:* Tests whether the student connects the Water Tower explanation and the Thar Desert water-scarcity explanation as a comparative pair.
> *Distractor A:* Indicates the student used infrastructure-and-migration reasoning rather than physical geography — a common shift toward familiar social explanation.
> *Distractor B:* Indicates partial engagement — the student recalls extreme temperatures but overstates, missing that people do live successfully in the Thar through adaptation.
> *Distractor D:* Indicates the student substituted policy reasoning for geographical reasoning — plausible to a student who has not engaged with the physical evidence.
> *Differentiation note:* A student who struggled with the map in Period 1 may still access this question if she engaged with the Period 3 contrast activity — look for whether her justification names meltwater specifically.
> *Oral follow-up:* "What would happen to the Gangetic Plains in a year when Himalayan glaciers did not melt at all?"

---

**Short Constructed Response 1**

Look at the following claim:
*"The direction a river flows tells you something important about the shape of the land it crosses."*

Using the Peninsular Plateau as your example, explain this claim in three to four sentences. Name at least two rivers and state which direction they flow and why.

*Expected response elements:*
- Identifies that the Peninsular Plateau tilts eastward.
- States that rivers like the Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri, or Mahanadi flow east toward the Bay of Bengal because of this tilt.
- Notes that the Narmada and Tapti are exceptions — they flow west into the Arabian Sea.
- Offers a logical inference: river direction is a map of land slope.

> **Annotation (Full)**
> *LO citation:* Period 4 — "…annotate a map of the Peninsular Plateau and coastlines showing river-flow direction, and explain in writing the geographical reason rivers flow differently on each side."
> *Competency link:* C-7.2 — reading topographical evidence to explain landform character.
> *Spectrum position:* Understanding-to-Application — student must restate the tilt-flow logic in explanatory prose, not just recall.
> *Purpose:* Tests whether the student can use the plateau's tilt as a causal explanation rather than a memorised fact.
> *Distractor meanings:* A student who lists rivers without explaining the tilt shows recall without application; a student who states rivers flow east but cannot name the exceptions shows incomplete engagement with the Narmada/Tapti anomaly discussion.
> *Differentiation note:* Accept a labelled diagram with arrows in lieu of prose from a student who produced strong map work in Period 4 — the diagram is the same demonstration.
> *Oral follow-up:* "If the plateau tilted west instead of east, which sea would the Godavari flow into?"

---

**Extended Constructed Response**

The chapter opens with India described as part of a subcontinent defined by natural boundaries, and closes with the hills of the Northeast. In between, it surveys regions that look and function very differently.

*Write a response of five to seven sentences that answers this question:*
**How does India's topographical diversity work as a system — that is, how do different regions depend on, protect, or contrast with one another?**

Your response must refer to at least three named regions and use at least two of the following relationships: boundary/barrier, water-supply, tilt/drainage, age/stability.

*What to look for:*
- Uses three or more distinct named regions (not just names — uses their geographical characteristics).
- Deploys at least two of the specified relationship types with accuracy.
- Demonstrates a systemic view: shows regions in relation, not as isolated list items.
- Does not merely repeat chapter headings — the student's own synthesis language is present.
- Strong responses will include a region that surprised them and explain why the chapter's evidence changed their expectation.

> **Annotation (Full)**
> *LO citation:* Period 1 — "…locate and label India's five regions and justify at least one way each natural boundary shapes the region it borders"; Period 4 — "…explain in writing the geographical reason rivers flow differently on each side"; Period 6 — "…produce a written explanation using evidence from at least three chapter regions to support the claim that India's topographical diversity reflects both geological processes and human adaptation."
> *Competency link:* C-7.2 — systematic discovery of topographical diversity as an integrated whole.
> *Spectrum position:* Analysis-to-Evaluation — student must organise multiple regional relationships into a coherent argument.
> *Purpose:* Tests whether the student has moved from listing regions to understanding them as a connected geographical system.
> *Distractor meanings:* A student who lists features region-by-region without showing relationships has engaged with individual sections but not with the integrative Period 6 synthesis task; a student who uses only two regions has not met the minimum for the competency's weight level.
> *Differentiation note:* A student who produced a strong Period 6 comparison table but struggles with extended prose may dictate her response or submit the annotated table with connective sentences added — the table itself demonstrates the systemic thinking.
> *Oral follow-up:* "Which relationship you described here was the hardest to see from just one region alone — and why did you need two regions to see it?"

---

**Open Task — Annotated Map**

*Task instructions for students:*

You have studied all the major geographical regions of India. Your task is to produce an annotated map that tells the story of how these regions are connected — not just where they are.

**What to do:**
1. Draw or use a printed outline map of India.
2. Mark and label all six regions covered in the chapter: Himalayas, Gangetic Plains, Thar Desert / Aravallis, Peninsular Plateau, Coastlines, and at least one island group or the Northeast Hills.
3. For each region, add one annotation box (two to three words) that names what the region *does* for the country — for example: "supplies river water," "blocks desert expansion," "drains east."
4. Draw at least two arrows or lines that show a *relationship* between two regions — label each arrow with a phrase that names the relationship (e.g., "melts into → feeds").
5. Add a legend explaining your arrow types.

**Partially completed scaffold:**
- Region already placed: *Himalayas* — marked in the north with annotation: "Water Tower / Natural Wall"
- One relationship arrow already drawn: Himalayas → Gangetic Plains — labelled "meltwater feeds"
- You must complete the remaining five regions, their annotations, and at least one more relationship arrow.

> **Annotation (Full)**
> *LO citation:* Period 1 — "…locate and label India's five geographical regions on an outline map and justify at least one way each natural boundary shapes the region it borders"; Period 4 — "…annotate a map showing river-flow direction and explain in writing the geographical reason rivers flow differently on each side"; Period 6 — "…produce a written explanation using evidence from at least three chapter regions to support the claim that India's topographical diversity reflects both geological processes and human adaptation."
> *Competency link:* C-7.2 — the open task is assigned to C-7.2 as the Central competency with the greatest number of implied LOs (Periods 1, 4, 6).
> *Open task format:* Annotated map — selected because the chapter is architecturally a regional survey and the activity sequence built map literacy across three periods; the map format makes topographical relationships spatially visible in a way prose cannot.
> *Spectrum position:* Analysis-to-Evaluation — student must decide which relationships are worth representing and how to label them accurately.
> *Purpose:* Tests whether the student can translate the systemic understanding developed in Period 6 into a spatial argument, moving beyond list-based recall.
> *Distractor meanings:* A map that places all regions correctly but has no relationship arrows shows strong locational recall without systemic thinking; a map with arrows but inaccurate labels (e.g., reversing the plateau's drainage direction) shows engagement with the relational task but incomplete content accuracy from Period 4.
> *Differentiation note:* A student who struggles with fine motor work for the map may produce the annotation boxes as a written list keyed to region numbers — the intellectual work is equivalent; accept this as a full response.
> *Oral follow-up:* "Looking at your map, which relationship arrow was the most difficult to decide on — why did you choose to draw it where you did?"

---

### Substantive Competency — C-6.1
*(Explaining key natural phenomena: tectonic processes, glacial rivers, landform formation)*

---

**MCQ 3**

The Himalayas are still growing at approximately five millimetres per year. Which of the following correctly explains why?

A. Glacial meltwater is depositing new material at the mountain peaks, building them upward.
B. ★ The Indian tectonic plate continues to push into the Eurasian plate, and the ongoing collision keeps folding the land upward.
C. The Shivalik Hills are eroding and the material is being lifted to the Greater Himalayas by wind.
D. The Himalayas grow because snow accumulation each winter adds mass to the peaks.

> **Annotation (Standard)**
> *LO citation:* Period 2 — "Students will be able to construct a comparative table of the three Himalayan ranges and use it to justify a human settlement decision with evidence from the chapter."
> *Competency link:* C-6.1 — explaining natural phenomena through tectonic process reasoning.
> *Distractor A:* Indicates the student conflated glacial deposition (a water process) with mountain formation (a tectonic process) — both involve the Himalayas but operate on entirely different mechanisms.
> *Distractor C:* Indicates the student applied a transfer-of-material logic without understanding that folding (not redistribution) drives mountain growth.
> *Distractor D:* Indicates the student confused mass addition through snowfall with structural growth — a plausible surface inference from the "Water Tower" framing.

---

**MCQ 4**
